elements.sent-2411-001: A semiring generalises the notion of a ring, replacing the additive abelian group structure with that of a commutative monoid.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this paper, we study a notion positioned between a ring and a semiring --- a semiring whose additive monoid is a commutative inverse semigroup.
elements.sent-2411-003: These inverse semirings include some important classes of semiring, as well as some new motivating examples.
elements.sent-2411-004: We devote particular attention to the inverse semiring of bounded polynomials and argue for their computational significance.
elements.sent-2411-005: We then prove a number of fundamental results about inverse semirings, their modules and their ideals.
elements.sent-2411-006: Parts of the theory show strong similarities with rings, while other parts are akin to the theory of idempotent semirings or distributive lattices.
elements.sent-2411-007: We note in particular that downward-closed submodules are precisely kernels.
elements.sent-2411-008: We end by exploring a connection to the theory of E-unitary inverse semigroups.
elements.sent-2411-009: Rings and distributive lattices share a number of properties and both fall under the umbrella of semirings --- a very general algebraic structure in which the additive group structure of a ring has been replaced by a commutative monoid.
elements.sent-2411-010: However, both of these examples already occur in a more restrictive class of semiring, which has a weak notion of `additive inverse' in the following sense.
elements.sent-2411-011: An inverse monoid is a monoid $(M,+,0)$ for which each $x \in M$ has a unique `inverse' element $-x$ such that $x + (-x) + x = x$ and $(-x) + x + (-x) = -x$.
elements.sent-2411-012: An inverse semiring is a semiring $(R,+,0, \cdot, 1)$ in which the additive monoid $(R,+,0)$ is an inverse monoid.
elements.sent-2411-013: Inverse semirings have appeared before in the literature under various names.
elements.sent-2411-014: They were introduced in \cite{Kar74 , where they were called additively inversive semirings.}
elements.sent-2411-015: They have also been called inverse semiring \cite{AD21 , additively inverse semiring \cite{dadhwal2024study , additive inverse semiring \cite{SM04 , and additively regular semiring \cite{Kar74 .}}}}
elements.sent-2411-016: Pure ideals and pure $k$-ideals of inverse semirings have been studied in \cite{palakawong2023characterizations , semiprime inverse semirings in \cite{sara2016centralizer,dog2021centralizers and the special class of so-called Clifford semirings in \cite{sen2005clifford, bhuniya2014clifford .}}}
elements.sent-2411-017: Derivations of various types in inverse semirings are studied in \cite{yilmaz2023note, dadhwal2024study, yaqoub2021generalized .}
elements.sent-2411-018: Despite this rather extensive list of papers, the basic theory and motivation for inverse semirings does not seem to have been written down before.
elements.sent-2411-019: Every ring and every idempotent semiring is an example of an inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-020: In the former case, the inverse is given by the usual negation, while in the latter it is the identity.
elements.sent-2411-021: An example that does not lie in either of these classes is given by the endomorphism semiring of a commutative inverse monoid.
elements.sent-2411-022: Another important example is the inverse semiring of `bounded polynomials', which we claim captures the computational nature of polynomials more accurately than the usual ring of polynomials (see \cref{ex:polynomials ).}
elements.sent-2411-023: The theory of inverse semirings has much in common with that of rings and idempotent semirings.
elements.sent-2411-024: In particular, they come equipped with a natural order structure, and ideals which are downward-closed with respect to this order behave very much like ideals of rings.
elements.sent-2411-025: More precisely, these ideals are the subtractive ideals and appear as the kernels of homomorphisms of inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-026: Similar results are true of modules over an inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-027: On the other hand, an ideal is upward-closed exactly when it contains the set of additive idempotents.
elements.sent-2411-028: Rings and idempotent semirings are not the only possible sources of inspiration.
elements.sent-2411-029: In fact, many concepts and results concerning inverse semigroups naturally extend to inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-030: For instance, the set of additive idempotents of an inverse semiring will be a subtractive ideal precisely when the inverse semiring is E-unitary.
elements.sent-2411-031: Moreover, every E-unitary inverse semiring embeds into the product of a ring and an idempotent semiring (see \cref{thm:e_unitary_embedding ).}
elements.sent-2411-032: In the study of inverse semirings, we occasionally encounter some near misses --- that is, algebraic structures that are almost inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-033: In particular, we encounter a number of structures that are inverse semirings except that $0 \cdot x \ne 0$ in general.
elements.sent-2411-034: The fact that these examples have an additive identity is perhaps accidental, and it may be best to think of their additive structure as inverse semigroups rather than inverse monoids.
elements.sent-2411-035: The Riemann sphere from complex analysis constitutes one such example (see \cref{ex:reimann_sphere ).}
elements.sent-2411-036: There are a number of basic results concerning (commutative) inverse monoids that we intend to take for granted in this paper.
elements.sent-2411-037: For a proper introduction to this topic see \cite{lawson1998inverse .}
elements.sent-2411-038: In an inverse monoid, $x-x$ is an idempotent element and we write $0_x = x-x$ for the idempotent associated to $x$ in this way.
elements.sent-2411-040: Let $M$ be a monoid such that for every $x \in M$ there is a $y \in M$ with $xyx = x$.
elements.sent-2411-041: Then $M$ is an inverse monoid if and only if the idempotents in $M$ commute.
elements.sent-2411-042: Consequently, if we consider taking inverses to be a unary operation, we see that inverse monoids form a variety.
elements.sent-2411-043: The above proposition implies that we do not need to prove the uniqueness of inverses if the monoid is commutative.
elements.sent-2411-045: The set $E(M)$ of idempotents of $M$ forms a semilattice under addition.
elements.sent-2411-046: Let $M$ be a commutative inverse monoid and suppose $z \in E(M)$.
elements.sent-2411-047: The set $\G(z) = \{s \mid 0_s = z\}$ is an abelian group with identity $z$.
elements.sent-2411-048: It is possible to define an order of the elements of an inverse monoid.
elements.sent-2411-049: We consider the opposite of the traditional order in this paper since we consider the monoid operation to be additive instead of multiplicative, i.e., we view $E(M)$ as a $\vee$-semilattice.
elements.sent-2411-050: Then we define the canonical preorder on $M$ by $x \le y$ if there exists an idempotent $z$ such that $x + z = y$.
elements.sent-2411-051: In an inverse monoid, $x \le y$ if and only if $x + 0_y = y$.
elements.sent-2411-052: There are two main results concerning $0$ and the order.
elements.sent-2411-053: Firstly, nothing compares less than zero and secondly, only idempotents compare greater than $0$.
elements.sent-2411-054: In an inverse monoid, $x \le 0$ if and only if $x = 0$.
elements.sent-2411-055: In an inverse monoid, $0 \le x$ if and only if $x$ is an idempotent.
elements.sent-2411-056: We also make use of some basic concepts from the theory of semirings.
elements.sent-2411-058: A semiring $(R,+,0, \times ,1)$ has an `additive' commutative monoid $(R,+,0)$ and a `multiplicative' monoid $(R,\times,1)$ such that multiplication distributes over addition.
elements.sent-2411-059: We write $\Rig$ for the category of semirings, where the morphisms are the expected notion of semiring homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-060: We say a semiring is commutative if its multiplicative monoid is commutative and write $\CRig$ for the corresponding category.
elements.sent-2411-061: The category $\Ring$ of rings and $\CRing$ of commutative rings are subcategories of $\Rig$ and $\CRig$, respectively.
elements.sent-2411-062: Another important class of semirings is that of idempotent semirings.
elements.sent-2411-063: An idempotent semiring $(R,+,\times, 0,1)$ is a semiring in which $+$ is idempotent --- that is, $x + x = x$ for all $x$.
elements.sent-2411-064: We denote the category of idempotent semirings by $\IdemRig$.
elements.sent-2411-065: Finally, we write $\InvRig$ for the category of inverse semirings and $\InvCRig$ for the subcategory of commutative structures.
elements.sent-2411-066: In this paper we will be interested in modules and algebras over inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-067: Given a semiring $R$, a commutative monoid $(M,+,0)$ becomes a (left) $R$-module by equipping it with an action $R\times M\to M$, satisfying that for all $a,b\in R$ and $x,y\in M$ we have.
elements.sent-2411-068: If $R$ is a commutative semiring, then an $R$-algebra $A$ is an $R$-module equipped with a compatible semiring structure.
elements.sent-2411-069: Explicitly, it is a module $A$ with an unital associative bilinear multiplication such that $r\cdot (a b) = (r\cdot a) b = a (r \cdot b)$, where $r$ is a scalar in $R$, $\cdot$ denotes scalar multiplication, and the algebra multiplication is denoted by juxtaposition.
elements.sent-2411-070: Note that a commutative $R$-algebra $A$ is the same thing as an inverse semiring $A$ equipped with a semiring homomorphism from $R$ to $A$.
elements.sent-2411-071: Just as an inverse semigroup simultaneously generalises groups and semilattices, inverse semirings simultaneously generalise rings and idempotent semirings, the latter of which include distributive lattices and the other following examples.
elements.sent-2411-072: A quantale $(Q,\bigvee,0,\cdot,1)$ is a complete join-semilattice equipped with a distributive multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-073: Since join is idempotent, quantales are idempotent semirings.
elements.sent-2411-074: An important example is given by the tropical semiring $(\mathds{R _\infty,\min,\infty,+,0)$.}
elements.sent-2411-076: Do note that, somewhat confusingly, $+$ is the multiplication and $\min$ is the idempotent addition.
elements.sent-2411-077: There are a number of interesting examples that are neither rings nor idempotent semirings.
elements.sent-2411-078: Let $X$ be a commutative inverse monoid.
elements.sent-2411-079: Then the set of endomorphisms $\End(X)$ has the structure of an inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-080: Addition is computed pointwise and multiplication is given by function composition.
elements.sent-2411-081: If the inverse monoid $X$ is neither a group nor a semilattice, this inverse semiring will be neither a ring nor an idempotent semiring.
elements.sent-2411-082: All inverse semirings $R$ embed into $\End(R)$ by a version of Cayley's theorem for semirings.
elements.sent-2411-083: Another fundamental example stems from computational considerations about polynomials.
elements.sent-2411-084: A polynomial is typically represented on a computer by a finite list of coefficients.
elements.sent-2411-085: The length of this list gives an upper bound on the degree of the polynomial, but since the coefficients of the highest powers of $x$ might be zero, this bound is not necessarily tight.
elements.sent-2411-086: Note that in the case of real or complex coefficients, it is not possible to ensure that this bound is tight, since checking whether a real number is zero is undecidable.
elements.sent-2411-087: Term-by-term addition finds $p(x) + q(x)$ to be $0x^2 + x$.
elements.sent-2411-088: This polynomial has degree 1, but the computer cannot determine this and so cannot reduce the size of the array.
elements.sent-2411-089: Thus, the usual ring of polynomials is not actually a faithful model of this situation.
elements.sent-2411-090: A better model is given by the following inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-091: Let $\R[x]_B$ be the set of pairs $(p,n)$, where $p \in \R[x]$ and $n \in \N \sqcup \{-\infty\}$ such that $\mathrm{deg (p) \le n$ (where $0$ is defined to have degree = $-\infty$).}
elements.sent-2411-092: This has an inverse semiring structure given by.

elements.sent-2411-113: We can also obtain a surprisingly useful class of inverse semirings by adjoining zeros to rings.
elements.sent-2411-114: The functor $(-)_0 \colon \InvRig \to \InvRig$ sends an inverse semiring $R$ to the inverse semiring $R_0 = R \sqcup 0$, where the addition, multiplication and inverses are defined on $R$ as before, while the new $0$ is an identity for addition and an absorbing element with respect to multiplication.
elements.sent-2411-115: As for morphisms, $f\colon R \to S$ is sent to a map $f_0\colon R_0 \to S_0$ which acts on the old elements like $f$ does and sends the new $0$ to $0$.
elements.sent-2411-116: We will denote the zero of $R$ in $R_0$ by $\widehat{0}$ to distinguish it from the newly adjoined zero.

elements.sent-2411-118: In particular, applying this construction to the rings $\Z$ and $\R$ yields the inverse semirings $\Z_0$ and $\R_0$, which we will see more of later in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-119: There are a number of algebraic structures that are almost, but not quite, inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-120: In particular, these examples are non-absorptive in the sense that $0 \cdot x$ need not equal $0$.
elements.sent-2411-121: In fact, in all the natural examples we are aware of, $0 \cdot x = 0_x$.
elements.sent-2411-122: It is perhaps best to think of this as a variant of inverse semirings in which the additive structure might lack a zero --- that is, it forms only an inverse semigroup under addition, instead of an inverse monoid.
elements.sent-2411-123: We call these zeroless inverse semirings.
elements.sent-2411-124: While a zero element does exist in all the examples we consider, it will be useful to not require it to be preserved by homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-125: The functor $(-)_0$ from \cref{def:adjoin_zero can be understood as arising from the adjunction between zeroless inverse semirings and inverse semirings.}
elements.sent-2411-126: The forgetful functor from inverse semirings to zeroless inverse semirings has a left adjoint which freely adds a zero.
elements.sent-2411-127: Having discussed adjoining $0$, we now consider a notion of adjoining $\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-128: Given a ring $R$, let $R_\infty$ be $R$ with an additional $\infty$ element added, where the addition, multiplication and inverses are defined on elements of $R$ as before, and where $x + \infty = \infty$ and $x \cdot \infty = \infty = \infty \cdot x$ for all $x \in R_\infty$.
elements.sent-2411-129: If $R$ is a ring, $R_\infty$ is a zeroless inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-130: We now have the following example.
elements.sent-2411-131: The Riemann sphere $\C_\infty$, with the natural addition and multiplication but with $0 \cdot \infty = \infty$ is a non-absorptive inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-132: Of course, we can obtain a proper inverse semiring by adjoining $0$.
elements.sent-2411-133: We will see some more examples of zeroless semirings later in the paper.
elements.sent-2411-134: In this section, we establish some basic facts about inverse semirings.

elements.sent-2411-216: As discussed before, both rings and idempotent semirings are examples of inverse semirings and we can view these relationships as subcategory inclusions.

elements.sent-2411-233: Polynomial rings can be understood as free commutative algebras over a ring.
elements.sent-2411-234: In this section, we will discuss the analogous case of free algebras over an inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-235: The simplest case to consider is the free inverse semiring on the empty set --- that is, the initial inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-236: The initial inverse semiring is $\Z_0$ --- the ring of integers $\Z$ with a new zero adjoined (see \cref{def:adjoin_zero ).}
elements.sent-2411-237: Let $R$ be an arbitrary inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-238: Any map $f\colon \Z_0 \to R$ must send the unit $1$ of $\Z_0$ to the unit of $R$, must preserve addition and inverses, and must send $0$ to $0$.

elements.sent-2411-241: Note then that a commutative inverse semiring is the same thing as a commutative $\Z_0$-algebra.

elements.sent-2411-261: In this section, we explore the theory of modules over an inverse semiring.
elements.sent-2411-262: What should such a module be? Mimicking the approach for rings, we might say a module over an inverse semiring $R$ is a commutative inverse monoid $M$ equipped with a homomorphism from $R$ to $\End(M)$ (see \cref{endInvSR ).}
elements.sent-2411-263: Alternatively, since inverse semiring is a special kind of semiring, we could use the notion of a semiring-module.
elements.sent-2411-264: The difference between these is that in the second case, the underlying monoid of $M$ is not assumed to be inverse.
elements.sent-2411-265: However, the following result shows that these two approaches actually agree.
elements.sent-2411-266: Throughout this section, the terms `module' and `ideal' can be understood to mean `left module' and `left ideal', though the results for the right-sided versions are essentially the same.

elements.sent-2411-274: As is the case with rings, an inverse semiring $R$ is a module over itself and ideals of $R$ can be identified with submodules of this module.
elements.sent-2411-275: An important class of ideals in semirings is that of subtractive ideals, with two-sided subtractive ideals corresponding to kernels of semiring homomorphisms.
elements.sent-2411-276: The following definition is a generalisation from ideals (i.e.\ submodules of a semiring) to arbitrary submodules recalled here for the reader's convenience (see also \cite[Chapter 14]{Gol99 ).}
elements.sent-2411-278: A submodule $S$ of an $R$-module $M$ is called subtractive if whenever $x \in S$, $y \in M$ and $x+y\in S$, we have that $y \in S$.
elements.sent-2411-279: Note that for a ring $R$ every sub-$R$-module is subtractive.

elements.sent-2411-371: In this paper we have worked out the basic theory of inverse semirings and discussed the extent to which the theory of modules over inverse semirings resembles the theory of modules over rings.
elements.sent-2411-372: We have neglected up to now to consider the ways in which the theory of inverse semirings is similar to the theory of inverse semigroups.
elements.sent-2411-373: Various concepts from inverse semigroup theory are relevant to inverse semirings, but for this section we will only focus on one of them.
elements.sent-2411-374: It is not hard to see that the ideal of idempotents $E(R)$ of an inverse semiring $R$ is subtractive precisely when its additive inverse semigroup is E-unitary.
elements.sent-2411-375: An inverse semiring $R$ is E-unitary if its underlying additive semigroup is E-unitary --- that is, if $x \in R$ and $z \in E(R)$, then $x + z$ is an idempotent if and only if $x$ is an idempotent.
elements.sent-2411-376: All rings, all idempotent semirings, and the inverse semirings of bounded polynomials are E-unitary.
elements.sent-2411-377: In addition, whenever an inverse monoid $X$ is E-unitary, the associated endomorphism inverse semiring $\End(X)$ will also be E-unitary.
elements.sent-2411-378: An important theorem of inverse semigroups states that an inverse semigroup $S$ is E-unitary if and only if it can be embedded into a semidirect product of a group and a semilattice (see \cite[Theorem 7.1.5]{lawson1998inverse ).}
elements.sent-2411-379: When $S$ is commutative, as in our case, the semidirect product reduces to a product.
